test_text.title: School Visit to Hinchingbrooke Country Park

audioscript.text: Part 1, you will hear a teaching assistant calling a country park about a school visit.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Good morning, Hinchingbrooke Country Park, Sally speaking.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I'm one of the rangers.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Oh, hello, uh, my name is John Chapman, and I'm a teaching assistant at a local primary school.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I've been asked to arrange a visit to the park for two of our classes.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: OK, what would you like to know?
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Well, I'm new to this area.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: So perhaps you could tell me something about the park first, please.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Of course.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Altogether the park covers 170 acres.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That's 69 hectares.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There are three main types of habitat, wetland, grassland, and woodland.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The woods are well established and varied.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: With an oak plantation and other areas of mixed species.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: The wetland is quite varied too.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The original farmland was dug up around 40 years ago to extract gravel.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Once this work was completed, the gravel pits filled with water, forming the two large lakes.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There are also several smaller ones.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Ponds and a stream that flows through the park.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: OK, so I suppose with these different habitats, there's quite a variety of wildlife.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: There certainly is.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: A lot of different species of birds and insects, and also animals like deer and rabbits.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And I understand you organize educational visits for school parties.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: We can organize a wide range of activities, and adapt them to suit all ages.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Can you give me some examples of the activities?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, one focus is on science, where we help children to discover and study plants, trees and insects.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: They also collect and analyze data about the things they see.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Another focus is on geography.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The park is a great environment to learn and practice reading a map.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And using a compass to navigate around the park.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Do you do anything connected with history?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Yes, we do.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: For instance, the children can explore how the use of the land has changed over time.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Then there's leisure and tourism.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That focuses on your visitors, I would imagine.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Yes, mostly.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: The children find out about them, their requirements, the problems they may cause, and how we manage these.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And another subject we cover is music.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Here the children experiment with natural materials to create sounds and explore rhythm and tempo.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That must be fun.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Most children really enjoy it.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: And of course, all the activities are educational too.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Learning outside the classroom encourages children to be creative and to explore and discover for themselves.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: I would imagine they get a sense of freedom that might not be a normal part of their lives.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And very often the children discover that they can do things they didn't know they could do.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: And they develop new skills.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: This gives them greater self-confidence.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: It sounds great.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: So what about the practical side of it?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: How much does it cost for a full day visit?
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: We would expect to bring between 30 and 40 children
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: If there are over 30, it costs £4.95 for each child who attends on the day.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: We invoice you afterwards, so you don't pay for children who can't come because of sickness, for example.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: There's no charge for leaders and other adults, as many as you want to bring.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: That sounds very fair.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: Well, thanks for all the information.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: I'll need to discuss it with my colleagues, and I hope to get back to you soon to make a booking.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: We'll look forward to hearing from you.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Goodbye.
audioscript.speaker: SALLY

audioscript.text: Goodbye, and thank you.
audioscript.speaker: JOHN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: Practical issues

audioscript.text: Part 2, you will hear the chairman of Stanthorpe Twinning Association, which organizes the link between Stanthorpe in England and a town in France.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Talking to members about the year's events.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully, and answer questions 11 to 15.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: It's great to see so many members of the Twinning Association here tonight.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Since the twinning link between our two towns, Stanthorpe here in England, and Malatte in France was established, the relationship between the towns has gone from strength to strength.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Last month, 25 members of the association from Stanthorpe spent a weekend in Malatte.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our hosts had arranged a great program.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We learned how cheese is produced in the region, and had the chance to taste the products.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The theme park trip had to be cancelled, but we all had a great time on the final boat trip down the river.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That was the real highlight.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is a special year for the association, because it's 25 years since we were founded.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In Malatte, they're planning to mark this by building a footbridge in the municipal park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've been discussing what to do here, and we've decided to plant a Poplar tree in the museum gardens.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We considered buying a garden seat to put there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But the authorities weren't happy with that idea.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In terms of fund raising to support our activities, we've done very well.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Our pancake evening was well attended and made record profits, and everyone enjoyed the demonstration of French cookery, which was nearly as successful.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Numbers for our film show were limited because of the venue, so we're looking for somewhere bigger next year.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're looking forward to welcoming our French visitors here next week.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And I know that many of you here will be hosting individuals or families.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The coach from France will arrive at 5 pm on Friday.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Don't try to do too much that first evening, as they'll be tired.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So have dinner in the house or garden rather than eating out.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The weather looks as if it'll be OK, so you might like to plan a barbecue.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then the next morning's market day in town, and that's always a good place to stroll round.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: On Saturday evening, we'll all meet up at the football club.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Where once again we'll have Toby Sharp and his band performing English and Scottish country songs.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Toby will already be well known to many of you, as last year he organized our special quiz night.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And presented the prizes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the talk, you have some time to look at questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 16 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, on Sunday, we'll be taking our visitors to Farley House.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You may not all be familiar with it, so here's a map to help you.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: You can see the car park at the bottom of the map.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's an excellent farm shop in the grounds where our visitors can buy local produce.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's in the old stables, which is the first building you come to.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're built round a courtyard, and the shop's in the far corner on the left.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's also a small cafe on the right as you go in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I know that one or two of our visitors may not be all that mobile.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The main entrance to the house has a lot of steps, so you might want to use the disabled entry.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is on the far side of the house from the car park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Children will probably be most interested in the adventure playground.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That's at the northern end of the larger lake, in a bend on the path that leads to the lake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There's lots for children to do there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are a number of lovely gardens near the house.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The kitchen gardens are rectangular and surrounded by a wall.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're to the northeast of the house, quite near the smaller lake.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They're still in use, and have a great collection of fruit and vegetables.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Temple of the Four Winds is a bit more of a walk, but it's worth it.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Take the path from the car park, and go past the western sides of the stables and the house.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Then, when the path forks, take the right-hand path.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Go up there with the woods on your left, and the temple is right at the end.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are great views over the whole area.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: OK, so that's the...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Take the path from the car park, and go past the western sides of the stables and the house.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Then, when the path forks, take the right-hand path.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Go up there with the woods on your left, and the temple is right at the end.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are great views over the whole area.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: OK, so that's the...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: During the visit to Malatte, in France, members especially enjoyed
What will happen in Stanthorpe to mark the 25th anniversary of the Twinning Association?
options: A garden seat will be bought.

items.title: Which event raised most funds this year?
For the first evening with the French visitors host families are advised to
options: take them for a walk round the town.

items.title: On Saturday evening there will be the chance to

items: Farm shop                    <input>
Disabled entry               <input>
Adventure playground         <input>
Kitchen gardens              <input>
The Temple of the Four Winds <input>

test_text.title: Colin and Marie's Discussion on Food Trends, Projects and Innovations

audioscript.text: Part 3, you will hear two food science students called Marie and Colin discussing their final year projects.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I haven't seen you for a bit, Marie.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: No, I've been busy with my project.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: You're making a vegan alternative to eggs, aren't you?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Something that doesn't use animal products.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: I'm using chickpeas.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: I had two main aims when I first started looking for an alternative to eggs, but actually I found chickpeas have got more advantages.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But how about your project on reusing waste food?
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: You were looking at bread, weren't you?
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's been hard work, but I've enjoyed it.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: The basic process was quite straightforward, breaking the stale bread down to a paste, then reforming it.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: But you were using 3D printing, weren't you, to make the paste into biscuits?
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I'd used that before, but in this project, I had time to play around with different patterns for the biscuits, and finding how I could add fruit and vegetables to make them a more appetizing color.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And I was really pleased with what I managed to produce.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: It must have been a great feeling to make something appetizing out of bits of old bread that would have been thrown away otherwise.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: It was.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And I'm hoping that some of the restaurants in town will be interested in the biscuits.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I'm going to send them some samples.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: I came across something on the internet yesterday that might interest you.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: It was a company that's developed touch sensitive sensors for food labels.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. It's a special sort of label on the food package.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: When the label's smooth, the food is fresh.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: And then when you can feel bumps on the label, that means the food's gone bad.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: It started off as a project to help visually impaired people.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: To know whether food was fit to eat or not.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Interesting.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: So just solid food?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: No, things like milk and juice as well.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: But actually I thought it might be really good for drug storage in hospitals and pharmacies.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And coming back to food, maybe it would be possible to use it for other things besides freshness.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Like how many kilograms a joint of meat is, for example?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Yes, there's all sorts of possibilities.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: I was reading an article about food trends, predicting how eating habits might change in the next few years.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Oh, things like more focus on local products.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: That seems so obvious, but the shops are still full of imported foods.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Yes, they need to be more proactive to address that, and somehow motivate consumers to change, yes.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: One thing everyone's aware of is the need for a reduction in unnecessary packaging, but just about everything you buy in supermarkets is still covered in plastic.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: The government needs to do something about it.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Absolutely.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: It's got to change.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Do you think there'll be more interest in gluten and lactose free food?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: For people with allergies or food intolerances.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: I don't know, lots of people I know have been buying that type of food for years now.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Yes, even if they haven't been diagnosed with an allergy.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: One thing I've noticed is the number of branded products related to celebrity chefs.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: People watch them cooking on TV.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: And then buy things like spice mixes or frozen foods with the chef's name on.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: I bought something like that once, but I won't again.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Yeah, I bought a ready made spice mix for chicken, which was supposed to be used by a chef I'd seen on television.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: And it didn't actually taste of anything.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Did the article mention ghost kitchens used to produce takeaway food?
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: No, what are they?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Well, they might have the name of a restaurant, but actually they're a cooking facility just for delivery meals.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: The public don't ever go there.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: But people aren't aware of that, it's all kept very quiet.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: So, people don't realize the food's not actually from the restaurant?
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Did you know more and more people are using all sorts of different mushrooms now to treat different health concerns, uh, things like heart problems.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Hmm, they might be taking a big risk there.
audioscript.speaker: MARIE

audioscript.text: Yes, it's hard to know which varieties are safe to eat.
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: Anyway, maybe now we should...
audioscript.speaker: COLIN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: Which TWO things did Colin find most satisfying about his bread reuse project?
options: finding a good way to prevent waste
overcoming problems in a basic process

body.title: Which TWO ways do the students agree that touch-sensitive sensors for food labels could be developed in future?
options: to show that food is no longer fit to eat
for use with drinks as well as foods
to provide applications for blind people
to indicate the weight of certain foods

text: What is the students' opinion about each of the following food trends?
textReadable: What is the students' opinion about each of the following food trends?

list: This is only relevant to young people.
This already seems to be widespread.
Retailers should do more to encourage this.
More financial support is needed for this.
Most people know little about this.
There should be stricter regulations about this.
items: Use of local products                              <input>
Reduction in unnecessary packaging                 <input>
Gluten-free and lactose-free food                  <input>
Use of branded products related to celebrity chefs <input>
Development of 'ghost kitchens' for takeaway food  <input>
Use of mushrooms for common health concerns        <input>
body.title: Food Trends

test_text.title: The Céide Fields: A Neolithic Site in Ireland

audioscript.text: Part 4, you will hear an archaeology student giving a presentation on an important site in Ireland called the Céide Fields.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: For my presentation today, I'm going to talk about the Céide Fields in the northwest of Ireland, one of the largest Neolithic sites in the world.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I recently visited this site and observed the work that is currently being done by a team of archaeologists there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The site was first discovered in the 1930s by a local teacher, Patrick Caulfield.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He noticed that when local people were digging in the bog, they were constantly hitting against what seemed to be rows of stones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He realized that these must be walls, and that they must be thousands of years old for them to predate the bog which subsequently grew over them.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He wrote to the National Museum in Dublin to ask them to investigate, but no one took him seriously.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It wasn't until 40 years later, when Patrick Caulfield's son Seamus, who had become an archaeologist by then, began to explore further.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: He inserted iron probes into the bog, to map the formation of the stones.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A traditional method which local people had always used for finding fuel buried in the bog for thousands of years.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Carbon dating later proved that the site was over 5,000 years old, and was the largest Neolithic site in Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Thanks to the bog which covers the area, the remains of the settlement at Céide fields, which is over 5,000 years old, are extremely well preserved.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A bog is 90% water, its soil so saturated that when the grasses and heathers that grow on its surface die, they don't fully decay, but accumulate in layers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Objects remain so well preserved in these conditions because of the acidity of the peat, and the deficiency of oxygen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At least 175 days of rain a year are required for this to happen.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This part of Ireland gets an average of 225 days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The Neolithic farmers at Céide would have enjoyed several centuries of relative peace and stability.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Neolithic farmers generally lived in larger communities than their predecessors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: With a number of houses built around a community building.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As they lived in permanent settlements, Neolithic farmers were able to build bigger houses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These weren't round as people often assume, but rectangular, with a small hole in the roof that allowed smoke to escape.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This is one of many innovations, and indicates that the Neolithic farmers were the first people to cook indoors.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another new technology that Neolithic settlers brought to Ireland was pottery.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Fragments of Neolithic pots have been found in Céide and elsewhere in Ireland.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The pots were used for many things, as well as for storing food.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Pots were filled with a small amount of fat, and when this was set alight, they served as lamps.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's thought that the Céide fields were mainly used as paddocks for animals to graze in.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Evidence from the Céide fields suggests that each plot of land was of a suitable size, to sustain an extended family.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Uh. They may have used a system of rotational grazing in order to prevent overgrazing and to allow for plant recovery and regrowth.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This must have been a year-round activity, as no structures have been found which would have been used to shelter animals in the winter.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: However, archaeologists believe that this way of life at Céide ceased abruptly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Why was this?
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Well, several factors may have contributed to the changing circumstances.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The soil would have become less productive, and led to the abandonment of farming.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The crop rotation system was partly responsible for this, as it would have been very intensive and was not sustainable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But there were also climatic pressures too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The farmers at Céide would have enjoyed a relatively dry period, but this began to change, and the conditions became wetter as there was a lot more rain.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It was these conditions that encouraged the bog to form over the area, which survives today.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So now I'd like to show you some...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: That is the end of the listening test.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: The pots were used for many things, as well as for storing food.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Pots were filled with a small amount of fat, and when this was set alight, they served as lamps.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's thought that the Céide fields were mainly used as paddocks for animals to graze in.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Evidence from the Céide fields suggests that each plot of land was of a suitable size, to sustain an extended family.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Uh. They may have used a system of rotational grazing in order to prevent overgrazing and to allow for plant recovery and regrowth.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This must have been a year-round activity, as no structures have been found which would have been used to shelter animals in the winter.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: However, archaeologists believe that this way of life at Céide ceased abruptly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Why was this?
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Well, several factors may have contributed to the changing circumstances.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The soil would have become less productive, and led to the abandonment of farming.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The crop rotation system was partly responsible for this, as it would have been very intensive and was not sustainable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But there were also climatic pressures too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The farmers at Céide would have enjoyed a relatively dry period, but this began to change, and the conditions became wetter as there was a lot more rain.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It was these conditions that encouraged the bog to form over the area, which survives today.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So now I'd like to show you some...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: That is the end of the listening test.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: In the IELTS test, you would now have 10 minutes to transfer your answers to the listening answer sheet.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER
